```{r finding adj-noun phases}
top_adj_noun_phraese <- adj_noun_phrases %>%
  count(phrase, sort = TRUE) %>% 
  head(20)
top_adj_noun_phraese
```

To enhance the signal-to-noise ratio, a filtering layer was applied to remove non-informative words. This ensures that the final visualization focuses on high-density semantic pairs that represent actionable consumer insights.

```{r filtering adj-noun phases}
# Remove generic/meta phrases (e.g., "other reviewer")
generic_nouns <- c("review", "reviewer", "people", "person", "time", "thing",
                   "stuff", "brand", "one", "pair", "lot", "usual", "medium")
generic_adjs  <- c("other", "first", "last", "many", "several", "same", "overall")

adj_noun_phrases_clean <- adj_noun_phrases %>%
  filter(!(noun %in% generic_nouns)) %>%
  filter(!(adj  %in% generic_adjs))

#Too many adjective noun phases are in the document. So filtering it can remove noise as spare terms or very frequent terms

# Filter too-rare and too-common phrases

phrase_counts <- adj_noun_phrases_clean %>%
  count(phrase, sort = TRUE)

n_reviews <- n_distinct(adj_noun_phrases_clean$Review_id)

adj_noun_phrases_filtered <- adj_noun_phrases_clean %>%
  inner_join(phrase_counts, by = "phrase") %>%
  filter(n >= 20) %>%                      # remove very rare
  filter(n <= 0.02 * n_reviews)            # remove too common (2% of reviews)

head(adj_noun_phrases_filtered)
```


```{r sentiment analysis using adj-noun phases}

# Ensure matching Review_id types
df_sentiment <- df_sentiment %>%
  mutate(Review_id = as.character(Review_id))

#tfidf calculation for adj-noun phrases
phr_tfidf <- adj_noun_phrases_filtered %>%
  left_join(df_sentiment %>% select(Review_id, bing_sentiment), by = "Review_id") %>%
  filter(!is.na(bing_sentiment)) %>%
  count(bing_sentiment, phrase, sort = TRUE) %>%
  bind_tf_idf(phrase, bing_sentiment, n) %>%
  arrange(bing_sentiment, desc(tf_idf))

  
# Top 15 phrases per bing sentiment group
top_phrases_adj <- phr_tfidf %>%
  mutate(sentiment_group = case_when(
    bing_sentiment > 0 ~ "positive",
    bing_sentiment < 0 ~ "negative",
    bing_sentiment == 0 ~ "neutral"
  )) %>%
  group_by(sentiment_group ) %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, n = 8, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

top_phrases_adj %>%
  select(phrase, sentiment_group, tf_idf)

```

```{r plot adj-noun phrase with sentiment}

ggplot(top_phrases_adj[1:30,],
       aes(x = reorder(phrase,tf_idf), y = tf_idf , fill = sentiment_group)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~  sentiment_group, scales = "free") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Distinctive Adjective–Noun Phrases by Sentiment",
    x = NULL,
    y = "TF-IDF Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

```

1.The Paradox of Price: Emphasize that "Great Price" is your most sensitive attribute. It drives both the highest satisfaction and the deepest disappointment, meaning customers are price-conscious but not "quality-blind.

2."Aesthetic vs. Utility: Points like "beautiful dress" are strong, but utility phrases like "skinny jeans" and "large bust" are weighted negatively, signaling that the brand's styling is beating its sizing.




```{r reco pos}
phrase_by_reco <- adj_noun_phrases_filtered %>%
  mutate(Recommended.IND = as.factor(Recommended.IND)) %>%
  count(Recommended.IND, phrase, sort = TRUE)

phrase_by_reco %>%
  group_by(Recommended.IND) %>%
  slice_max(n, n = 10, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

```

```{r top word for reco by pos plot}
top_reco_word <- phrase_by_reco %>%
  group_by(Recommended.IND) %>%
  slice_max(n, n = 10, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(top_reco_word, aes(x = reorder(phrase, n), y = n, fill = Recommended.IND)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = TRUE) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ Recommended.IND, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(title = "Top ADJ+NOUN Phrases by Recommendation", x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

```
Phrase-level analysis shows that positive recommendations are driven by expressions such as “normal size”, “skinny jeans”, and “high quality”, highlighting the importance of accurate sizing and product quality. In contrast, non-recommended reviews frequently mention “smaller size”, “larger size”, and “extra fabric”, indicating dissatisfaction with fit and construction. This supports earlier findings that fit accuracy is a key driver of customer satisfaction.


```{r neg phrase}

# Identifying occurrences of the word 'not' and capturing the immediate subsequent lemma to reconstruct the negative sentiment.
neg_phrases <- anno_df %>%
  group_by(doc_id, sentence_id) %>%# Ensuring tokens are in original sentence order before identifying the 'next' word in the sequence.
  arrange(token_id, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(next_lemma = lead(lemma)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(tolower(token) == "not", !is.na(next_lemma)) %>% # Isolating bigrams where the modifier is 'not' and a target word exists.
  transmute(
    Review_id = as.character(doc_id),
    Department.Name, Rating, Recommended.IND, Age,
    phrase = str_to_lower(paste("not", next_lemma))
 ) %>%
  #Ensuring each unique negation phrase is counted only once per review.
  distinct(Review_id, phrase, .keep_all = TRUE)

# Identifying the top 20 most frequent 'not' phrases to pinpoint the most common areas of consumer dissatisfaction.
neg_phrases %>% 
  count(phrase, sort = TRUE) %>% 
  head(20)

```


```{r filtering negation phrase}

# Feature Refinement: Filtering the negation phrases to ensure the remaining bigrams represent meaningful product attributes.
generic_neg <- c("too", "a", "be", "the","do", "in", "have", "to", "at", "as","even", "so", "for", "what", "it", ".")

neg_phrases_clean <- neg_phrases %>%
  filter(!str_remove(phrase, "not ") %in% generic_neg)


phrase_counts <- neg_phrases_clean %>%
  count(phrase, sort = TRUE)

n_reviews <- n_distinct(adj_noun_phrases_clean$Review_id)

neg_phrases_clean_filtered <- neg_phrases_clean %>%
  inner_join(phrase_counts, by = "phrase") %>%
  filter(n >= 20) %>%                      # remove very rare
  filter(n <= 0.02 * n_reviews)            # remove too common (2% of reviews)

```



```{r top filtered neg phrase}

# TF-IDF by Bing sentiment
df_sentiment <- df_sentiment %>%
  mutate(Review_id = as.character(Review_id))

phr_tfidf <- neg_phrases_clean_filtered %>%
  left_join(df_sentiment %>% select(Review_id, bing_sentiment), by = "Review_id") %>%
  filter(!is.na(bing_sentiment)) %>%
  count(bing_sentiment, phrase, sort = TRUE) %>%
  bind_tf_idf(phrase, bing_sentiment, n) %>%
  arrange(bing_sentiment, desc(tf_idf))

# Top 15 phrases per sentiment group
top_phrases_neg <- phr_tfidf %>%
  mutate(sentiment_group = case_when(
    bing_sentiment > 0 ~ "positive",
    bing_sentiment < 0 ~ "negative",
    bing_sentiment == 0 ~ "neutral"
  )) %>%
  group_by(sentiment_group ) %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, n = 10, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

top_phrases_neg %>%
  select(phrase, sentiment_group, tf_idf)
```


```{r plot top neg phrase}
ggplot(top_phrases_neg,
       aes(x = reorder(phrase,tf_idf), 
           y = tf_idf , 
           fill = sentiment_group)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~  sentiment_group, scales = "free") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Distinctive Adjective–Noun Phrases by Sentiment",
    x = NULL,
    y = "TF-IDF Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The data reveals a "Negation Paradox": while "not work" and "not flatter" are clear markers of product failure in the Negative category, phrases like "not disappoint" and "not wait" dominate the Positive category as idiomatic expressions of high satisfaction. The Neutral tier is defined by "uncertainty" phrases like "not sure" and "not really," pinpointing a segment of underwhelmed customers who are at risk of churning.


## Conclusion
Overall, this study integrates multiple text mining and analytical techniques to develop a holistic understanding of customer experiences in retail reviews. LDA topic modelling reveals broad, coherent themes and shows that experiential topics such as style, wearability, and retail experience are consistently associated with high ratings and positive sentiment. NMF further refines these insights by producing more interpretable, parts-based topics that emphasise distinctive issues related to fit, fabric quality, and post-purchase experience. STM extends this analysis by linking topics to customer behaviour, demonstrating that comfort and accurate fit significantly increase the likelihood of recommendation, while delivery and return issues drive dissatisfaction. Regression analysis confirms the predictive importance of these themes, and POS-based phrase extraction provides fine-grained interpretability by revealing how customers explicitly express praise and complaints in their own words.

Taken together, the findings consistently highlight fit accuracy, comfort, and perceived quality as the strongest drivers of customer satisfaction, while shortcomings in online representation and returns emerge as the primary sources of dissatisfaction, offering clear and actionable directions for product and service improvement.
